title: Parenting from Conception Cont'd
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 01-28-24 - The Faithful Parent 7 - Parenting from Conception Cont’d - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Responding to the Sanctity of Human Life
subpoints: Every human from conception bears the image of God
Abortion is the unjust taking of innocent life and constitutes murder
The Church must repent of its indifference to the plight of the unborn
Christians have a spiritual duty to work against this evil through prayer, voting, volunteering, and advocacy
The ultimate solution to abortion is the gospel of Jesus Christ

outline.title: Foundation Expectation 1: The Bible Is Sufficient for Parenting
subpoints: The Bible addresses every moral and principled question in child rearing
Scripture equips the man of God for every good work, including parenting
Christian parents need not look beyond Scripture for parenting wisdom
Reliance on secular psychology undermines the sufficiency of God's Word

outline.title: Foundation Expectation 2: Discipline Cannot Replace Regeneration
subpoints: Every child is conceived dead in trespasses and sins
Strict discipline can channel sin but cannot change the heart
The goal of parenting must be changed hearts, not merely well-behaved children
Children of believers are covenant members, yet still need Christ
Parents must always point children toward repentance and faith

outline.title: Foundation Expectation 3: Godly Child Rearing Is Covenantal
subpoints: Children of at least one believing parent are covenantally sanctified
Covenant children occupy a special relationship with God
Parents who fulfill covenant obligations have reason to expect their children's salvation
God's mercy extends to children's children of those who fear Him

outline.title: Foundation Expectation 4: The Father Bears Final Parenting Responsibility
subpoints: The Bible places primary responsibility for child rearing on the father
Fathers must take initiative—they cannot abdicate leadership to their wives
Godly fatherhood requires sacrifice and servant leadership modeled on Christ
Honoring the mother's strengths and dignity is essential to godly fatherhood
A healthy marriage is foundational to effective parenting

outline.title: Foundation Expectation 5: Young Children Need Guidance, Not Independence
subpoints: Young children are not equipped for independence and need structure
Early years of faithful parenting spare much grief in later years
Many parents reverse the proper order—too much freedom early, too much control later
Young children are exasperated by too much liberty; older children by too much control

summary:
This Sunday School lesson is the seventh installment in the Faithful Parent series and picks up where the previous lesson left off on parenting from conception. Pastor Lyro begins by completing the earlier session's discussion on the sanctity of human life, arguing that because every human bears the image of God from the moment of conception, abortion constitutes murder. He calls the Church to repent of its practical indifference to the unborn and to engage in concrete action—prayer, advocacy, voting, volunteering at pregnancy centers, and sharing the gospel—as spiritual duties rooted in Proverbs 24 and 31.

The lesson then transitions to five foundational expectations applicable to parenting at every stage. First, the Bible is fully sufficient for parenting; Christian parents need no outside psychological system to raise their children well. Second, discipline is not a substitute for regeneration—every child is born a sinner and must be pointed to Christ, not merely trained into external compliance. Third, Christian child rearing is covenantal: children of at least one believing parent hold a special covenantal status before God, giving parents grounds for prayerful confidence.

Fourth, Scripture places final parenting responsibility on the father, who cannot delegate or abdicate this calling. Fatherhood requires servant leadership modeled after Christ—authority exercised through sacrifice and initiative, never passive or coercive. Honoring and supporting the mother, maintaining a faithful marriage, and knowing what is happening in the household are all dimensions of this responsibility. Fifth, young children need boundaries, not independence; parents who establish firm, loving guidance early will face far less grief during the teenage years than those who defer discipline.
transcript:
## Introduction

We are continuing our series, San Francisco series, on the faithful parent. We have examined what the Scriptures say concerning parenting the infant, the toddler, the preschooler, the elementary age child. And then last week we also saw that parenthood starts at conception. And that from that moment on, the baby in the womb is a full human person and that there is consequences to that understanding. And we took a look at the sanctity of human life and the context of parenting and so on. And we ran out of time, we saw that human life is holy. That is different than the animal life we saw that that is that every human bears the image of God for the moment it comes off conception. Therefore, killing a child that is in the womb is murder because it's the unjust taking of innocent life. But we were not able to get to the what then.

If this, these things being true, what we do with this truth that abortion is actually murder is a taking of life of an innocent life. And so we're going to continue that today and then jump into. So it might be a little discombobulative as far as how the lesson goes because it's two different lessons. So we're going to finish one and kind of start the next one. And we're hopefully Lord willing to take a look at five foundation expectations that apply to all parenting.

## Responding to the Sanctity of Human Life

So we saw that all human life is sacred. It's holy. We saw that every human from a moment of conception on bears the image of God and that image of God has nothing to do with intellectual capability. But with the ability to know and be known by God, to be in a spiritual being that can know God as a father and the Lord Jesus Christ as Savior. So what do we do with that information?

I think the first thing we do and the first thing the Church of Jesus Christ needs to do is to repent from our sin of indifference. I think the Church of Jesus Christ has been very indifferent to the plight of the unborn. I think the Church of Jesus Christ in this country doesn't think this is a big deal. Especially since the Dobbs decision. Since the Dobbs decision, we've been thinking that the battle is over. And yet since the Dobbs decision abortion has increased in the United States. Every state that had a ballot measure concerning abortion, it passed in favor of abortion.

The second thing is that we must consider part of our spiritual duty to work against this evil in whatever way we righteously can. A famous passage in this area is Proverbs 24, 11 and 12. Where Solomon says, rescue those who are being taken away to death, hold back those who are stumbling to the slaughter. If you say behold, we did not know this. Does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? Does not he who keeps watch over your soul know it. And will he not repay them the payment according to his work?

Now, as important that we understand to whom the book of Proverbs is being written—he's not being written to the world out there. He's being written to the covenant community of God. And you see the duty here, the first word is rescue. And then later on says, hold back or deliver. Those who should do this—the covenant community of God. And those who should be rescued—those who are being taken away to death unjustly.

So what do we do? We pray. We open our mouth and speak about it. We vote. We attend hearings. We volunteer at crisis pregnancy centers. We learn how to counsel women from the Scriptures. We open our house to a pregnant woman who has nowhere to go. We consider adoption. We give money wisely. We go knock on our representative's door. But the ultimate solution for this issue really is the gospel of Jesus Christ. Laws are good, but they don't do much for the heart.

## Five Foundation Expectations for All Parenting

Now let's shift attention a little bit, and talk about five foundation expectations for fathers and mothers, at every stage of parenting.

### Expectation 1: The Bible Is Sufficient

First, in fulfilling parental duties and privilege, the Bible is sufficient. Bringing up children of all ages can be mystifying. But the Bible gives us every answer to every question that has to do with principles and morals, and the Bible is sufficient for that. The Apostle Paul talking to Timothy says that the word of God is profitable for instruction, for rebuke, so that the men of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work. Everything that God calls them to do—the means to arrive at that is the word of God.

### Expectation 2: Discipline Is No Substitute for Regeneration

Secondly, discipline is no substitute for every generation. You cannot spank your children into heaven. The Christian parents must also remember that discipline is no substitute for regeneration. Every child, no matter how cute or small or helpless he or she is, is a sinner. Strict discipline may channel that sin in socially acceptable ways, but that is all it can do apart from the regenerating work of God in that child. The Bible says we are all dead in trespasses and sins. By nature, children of wrath. The Christian parent must always take the reality of sin and rebellion into account. Parenting is not just to create good citizens, but to point children to Christ that they might know their Savior.

### Expectation 3: Godly Child Rearing Is Covenantal

Third, godly child rearing is covenantal. The children of believers, although they have the nature of sinners, have been given tremendous covenantal privileges. Paul says in 1 Corinthians 7, because of the believing parent, the children are holy. There's this covenantal aspect in parenting. The children of Christian parents are covenantally sanctified, even though their nature may not yet be changed through regeneration. When all the teaching of the Bible is taken into account, parents who fulfill their covenantal obligations have every reason to expect that their children will be saved. The Psalmist says the mercy of the Lord is from everlasting to everlasting on those who fear him, and his righteousness to children's children.

### Expectation 4: The Father Bears Final Responsibility

Fourth, it is absolutely essential for both parents to recognize that the final responsibility for child rearing is the father's. You cannot pass it on to the wife. Ephesians 6:4 says, fathers do not provoke your children to wrath, but raise them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord. Leadership necessarily involves initiative. In Ephesians 5:25 and 6:4, both the verbs love and bring up are active verbs in the active voice. The Bible tells us that in the home, the husband is the picture of Christ. Each husband every day is talking about Christ through his behavior.

Leadership also involves honoring and respecting the wife's limitations. Children are far more mindful of their father than their mother, thus the expression "wait till your father comes home." But child rearing is not a competition. The husband and wife are on the same team. Leadership in child rearing also involves honoring and respecting the mother's strengths and dignity. The husband must keep covenant with the wife of his youth, as Malachi makes clear.

And ultimately, godly leadership necessarily involves sacrifice. Jesus taught us that the way to be a godly leader involves servanthood. John 10 shows us the shepherd is both master and servant of the sheep. Parents have full authority, but it is the authority of servanthood.

### Expectation 5: Young Children Are Not Equipped for Independence

Fifthly, young children are not equipped for independence. If parents do well in the first five years then they will be spared much grief later. But we tend to do the opposite—we give a bunch of freedom to young children and then when they're teenagers we try to control them to no end. Young children are exasperated by too much liberty. Older children are exasperated by too much control. We have to make sure that we have those things in the right place in life.

## Closing Prayer

Father, have in thank you that you're good. We thank you that your word is sufficient. We pray that we cling to it through Christ and that we be faithful to it, in parenting, but in every area of our lives, for asking Jesus name, amen.
